EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to have blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Surface temperatures soared to the low to mid 90s in most areas, with dew point temperatures in the upper 70s to mid 80s.  This resulted in most unstable CAPE values of 5000-8000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -10 to -14 C range.  The LCL was relatively high in the 1000-1500 meter range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches.  Thunderstorms developed over the northwest part of Iowa by early afternoon.  The storms became severe and dropped southeast into the most unstable air to the east and south of the complex.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of high winds and hail.  Some of the hail reached golf ball in size, however with the warm atmospheric conditions much of the hail remained below severe levels.  High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread street flooding in the town of Waukee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down, some 2 to 4 feet in diameter.  Powerlines were down, a large tree fell and damaged a house foundation, and another large tree fell and damaged the roof of a house. Dime size hail fell too.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large 12 to 14 inch diameter tree down. Six inch diameter limbs down around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Surface temperatures soared to the low to mid 90s in most areas, with dew point temperatures in the upper 70s to mid 80s.  This resulted in most unstable CAPE values of 5000-8000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -10 to -14 C range.  The LCL was relatively high in the 1000-1500 meter range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches.  Thunderstorms developed over the northwest part of Iowa by early afternoon.  The storms became severe and dropped southeast into the most unstable air to the east and south of the complex.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of high winds and hail.  Some of the hail reached golf ball in size, however with the warm atmospheric conditions much of the hail remained below severe levels.  High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported in town, and one light pole was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed in western Nebraska during the late evening of June 22nd moved into western Iowa around noon on the 23rd.  The storms created an outflow boundary which collided with very unstable conditions, temperatures of 90 to 95 degrees and dew point temperatures in the upper 70s.  This caused new thunderstorm development along and east of the Missouri River north of Omaha by mid afternoon.  The storms were mainly wet micro-burst producers although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at close to 60 mph ripped the top part of a grain bin off near Modale spilling the corn stored inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and very humid conditions was observed over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on June 22nd and 23rd.  High temperatures on the 22nd were in the lower to mid 90s and in the mid to upper 90s on the 23rd.  Overnight lows on the 23rd were in the mid to upper 70s.  Dew point temperatures on the 23rd were in the mid 70s to lower 80s.  The combination of the heat and humidity brought heat index values up into the 108 to 118 degree range during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Since these extremely uncomfortable temperatures occurred with light winds, generally less than 10 mph, conditions became deadly for livestock, especially during the afternoon of the 23rd.  It was estimated that at least 2,000 head of cattle died because of the heat in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa, most of them on the 23rd.  Conditions improved a bit during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd when isolated thunderstorms and associated outflow brought a little cooler temperatures and increased winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph knocked down 6 to 8 inch in diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in the area, and the corn was blown down.

Rainfall was fairly evenly distributed through out the month but totaled much less than usual over northwestern and west central sections. Greatest rainfall came on the 26th and was centered upon central and north central Iowa. Ames reported 5.06 inches of rain between the evening of the 25th and morning of the 27th. However, relatively dry weather prior to this rain event prevented widespread flooding from this late month storm.  Much of the flooding was very minor with crests less than one half foot over flood stage.  The flooding on the West Fork of the Cedar River was more significant with the crest about 1.5 feet above flood stage.  A crest of a little over 2 feet above flood stage was recorded in Oskaloosa on the South Skunk River.
Heavy rains of 2 to 3 inches fell across parts of southeast IA during the evening and overnight hours of April 29 and 30.  Flooding was observed in the Burlington, IA area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains resulted in two separate landslides in Burlington, IA on the morning of April 30.  One resident on Sweeney Avenue lost a 20 foot by 10 foot section of his backyard while another resident on the 300 block of South 5th Street sustained damage to a 20 foot section of a retaining wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was fairly evenly distributed through the month, however there was a period of heavier rainfall that occurred in two rain events during the middle of the month.  The rain of 1 to 2 inches caused some relatively minor flooding over southern Iowa.  Flooding was relatively minor and mainly restricted to agricultural lands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time and location estimated.  A thunderstorm blew a tree down onto a power line around 10:30 pm Saturday, April 25 knocking the power out to about 757 Alliant Energy coustomers in Burlington.

A cold front that touched off isolated severe thunderstorms on April 24th and early on the 25th stalled over northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri on the 25th.  The front then returned north as a warm front during the morning of April 26th.  Temperatures south of the front climbed into the 70s with 40s and 50s prevailing to the north.  This contrast, combined with an upper level disturbance, helped kick off a line of thunderstorms north of the returning warm front.  The storms produced large hail as far north as the Omaha area and some damaging wind gusts in southwest Iowa east and southeast of Omaha.  In addition, with the storms tracking across the same area for many hours, some heavy rain fell over far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Doppler Radar estimated and cooperative observers reported that some locations received 3 inches or more for 24 hour rainfall amounts from the storm.  However, no significant flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph downed a tree, the trunk of which was 18 inches in diameter.  Several large branches were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorms produced large hail in Plymouth County during the very early morning of April 25th.

A weak cold front, possibly reinforced by thunderstorms that occurred either earlier in the afternoon or during the previous evening, caused a few severe thunderstorms to develop from southwest Iowa into southeast Nebraska.  Due to hot and humid conditions present over the region, the thunderstorms were mainly the wet micro-burst variety although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds were estimated to have reached at least 60 mph by a trained spotter.  The winds knocked some rotted trees down and were accompanied by pea-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass remained quite unstable across the state as a weak cold front approached from the northwest and the main short wave lifted northeast through the central U.S.  CAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg range with lifted indices between -8 and -9 C.  Thunderstorms formed over southern Iowa in the rich air with dew points in the mid 70s.  Downdraft CAPE was quite high at 1000 to 1200 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was quite a bit less than the previous round, in the 400 to 800 J/kg range.  The column was significantly sheared with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear present.  Plenty of moisture was available with 2.2 inches of precipitable water noted just south of the thunderstorm inception area.  Thunderstorms fired by early afternoon over a good part of Iowa with the strongest in the south central and parts of central Iowa.  Initially, the storms produced sub-severe hail around nickle size.  As the afternoon progressed, the storms became stronger and resulted in high winds.  Winds of 68 MPH hit the ASOS at Ottumwa for example.  In addition, the storms produced heavy rainfall.  This produced flooded roads in some areas, especially in Warren and Madison Counties.

Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches.  Thunderstorms developed over the northwest part of Iowa by early afternoon.  The storms became severe and dropped southeast into the most unstable air to the east and south of the complex.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of high winds and hail.  Some of the hail reached golf ball in size, however with the warm atmospheric conditions much of the hail remained below severe levels.  High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dallas County EM reports roof ripped off farm buildings and corn blown over.

A semi-stationary frontal boundary remained in place across southern Iowa while a secondary boundary was stretched from eastern South Dakota into northeast Iowa.  The airmass became very unstable with lifted indices falling to -10 to -15 C and most unstable CAPE rising to 5000 to 6000 J/kg.  Zero to 3 km CAPE rose to over 200 J/kg in northern Iowa by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose to the 80s to low 90s, as dew point values reached the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Wind fields were somewhat weak, however effective shear was in the 35 to 50 kt range by late afternoon.  The amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere rose to over 400 J/kg by late afternoon.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places initially.  The first was along the northern boundary.  However the severe storms with this round remained to the north of Iowa.  A second round formed north of the southern boundary along the edge of the mid level cap.  The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa.  The highest wind gust was 74 MPH, measured at an RWIS site near Ankeny in Polk County.  The wind damage was largely to trees and power lines, with spotty reports of small shed and minor building damage.  A semi-tractor trailer was blown off of Interstate 35 near Highway 210 in Story County.  The high winds ripped siding off of a house in Ankeny in Polk county, and windows were blown out of a house in Colfax in Jasper County.  Thunderstorms rekindled on the back side of the main thunderstorm area after sunrise.  A few storms became severe and produced 60 MPH winds and golf ball size hail as they moved from The Fort Dodge area into Story County.

The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage was reported.

The airmass became very unstable with lifted indices falling to -10 to -15 C and most unstable CAPE rising to 5000 to 6000 J/kg.  Zero to 3 km CAPE rose to over 200 J/kg in northern Iowa by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose to the 80s to low 90s, as dew point values reached the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Wind fields were somewhat weak, however effective shear was in the 35 to 50 kt range by late afternoon.  The amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere rose to over 400 J/kg by late afternoon.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places initially.  The first was along the northern boundary. The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-stationary frontal boundary remained in place across southern Iowa while a secondary boundary was stretched from eastern South Dakota into northeast Iowa.  The airmass became very unstable with lifted indices falling to -10 to -15 C and most unstable CAPE rising to 5000 to 6000 J/kg.  Zero to 3 km CAPE rose to over 200 J/kg in northern Iowa by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose to the 80s to low 90s, as dew point values reached the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Wind fields were somewhat weak, however effective shear was in the 35 to 50 kt range by late afternoon.  The amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere rose to over 400 J/kg by late afternoon.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places initially.  The first was along the northern boundary.  However the severe storms with this round remained to the north of Iowa.  A second round formed north of the southern boundary along the edge of the mid level cap.  The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa. The wind damage was largely to trees and power lines, with spotty reports of small shed and minor building damage. Thunderstorms rekindled on the back side of the main thunderstorm area after sunrise.  A few storms became severe and produced 60 MPH winds and golf ball size hail as they moved from The Fort Dodge area into Story County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees up to 15 inch in diameter were blown down.

The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa.  The highest wind gust was 74 MPH, measured at an RWIS site near Ankeny in Polk County.  The wind damage was largely to trees and power lines, with spotty reports of small shed and minor building damage.  A semi-tractor trailer was blown off of Interstate 35 near Highway 210 in Story County.  The high winds ripped siding off of a house in Ankeny in Polk county, and windows were blown out of a house in Colfax in Jasper County.  Thunderstorms rekindled on the back side of the main thunderstorm area after sunrise.  A few storms became severe and produced 60 MPH winds and golf ball size hail as they moved from The Fort Dodge area into Story County.

The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed in western Nebraska during the late evening of June 22nd moved into western Iowa around noon on the 23rd.  The storms created an outflow boundary which collided with very unstable conditions, temperatures of 90 to 95 degrees and dew point temperatures in the upper 70s.  This caused new thunderstorm development along and east of the Missouri River north of Omaha by mid afternoon.  The storms were mainly wet micro-burst producers although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accompanied by 40 mph winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was snapped near Mosquito Creek by thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass remained quite unstable across the state as a weak cold front approached from the northwest and the main short wave lifted northeast through the central U.S.  CAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg range with lifted indices between -8 and -9 C.  Thunderstorms formed over southern Iowa in the rich air with dew points in the mid 70s.  Downdraft CAPE was quite high at 1000 to 1200 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was quite a bit less than the previous round, in the 400 to 800 J/kg range.  The column was significantly sheared with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear present.  Plenty of moisture was available with 2.2 inches of precipitable water noted just south of the thunderstorm inception area.  Thunderstorms fired by early afternoon over a good part of Iowa with the strongest in the south central and parts of central Iowa.  Initially, the storms produced sub-severe hail around nickle size.  As the afternoon progressed, the storms became stronger and resulted in high winds.  Winds of 68 MPH hit the ASOS at Ottumwa for example.  In addition, the storms produced heavy rainfall.  This produced flooded roads in some areas, especially in Warren and Madison Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of water over County Road G68.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Surface temperatures soared to the low to mid 90s in most areas, with dew point temperatures in the upper 70s to mid 80s.  This resulted in most unstable CAPE values of 5000-8000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -10 to -14 C range.  The LCL was relatively high in the 1000-1500 meter range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches.  Thunderstorms developed over the northwest part of Iowa by early afternoon.  The storms became severe and dropped southeast into the most unstable air to the east and south of the complex.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of high winds and hail.  Some of the hail reached golf ball in size, however with the warm atmospheric conditions much of the hail remained below severe levels.  High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.

Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that has been in place across Iowa lifted north during the day on the 18th and had become nearly stationary across northeast Iowa.  The airmass warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s.  A significant cap was in place during the day into the evening of the 18th with 700 mb temperatures of +12 to +13 C.  Mid level temperatures cooled during the evening and overnight hours as a strong upper level trough approached the area.  The airmass remained very unstable into the early morning hours of the 19th, with CAPE in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range and lifted indices remained in the -5 to-7 C range.  Downdraft CAPE was high at 900 to 1300 J/kg.  There was significant CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere ranging from around 700 to 900 J/kg, however the freezing level was quite high at 14,000 feet.  In addition, 1.75 to 2 inches of precipitable water was available.  Thunderstorms formed in Nebraska and moved into Iowa.  The storms were heavy precipitation storms and combined with the warm atmosphere limited the storms in hail production.  Some sub-severe hail was reported.  The storms became a forward propagating MCS and wind was the primary mode of severe weather.  Tree and power line damage was widespread with winds of 60 MPH or higher common.  The wind event took place mainly from Interstate 80 north to the U.S. 30 corridor.  In eastern Marshall County, a mesonet station recorded a 94 MPH wind gust.  Damage was widespread across eastern Marshall County into Tama County.

A very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Surface temperatures soared to the low to mid 90s in most areas, with dew point temperatures in the upper 70s to mid 80s.  This resulted in most unstable CAPE values of 5000-8000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -10 to -14 C range.  The LCL was relatively high in the 1000-1500 meter range.  Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches.  Thunderstorms developed over the northwest part of Iowa by early afternoon.  The storms became severe and dropped southeast into the most unstable air to the east and south of the complex.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of high winds and hail.  Some of the hail reached golf ball in size, however with the warm atmospheric conditions much of the hail remained below severe levels.  High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.

Thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska during the night and early morning hours of the 24th.  The thunderstorms pushed northeast, then east into Iowa around sunrise.  The storms were elevated in nature by the time they reached Iowa.  One of the storms produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail as it moved through Calhoun and Webster Counties.

Thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska during the night and early morning hours of the 24th.  The thunderstorms pushed northeast, then east into Iowa around sunrise.  The storms were elevated in nature by the time they reached Iowa.  One of the storms produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail as it moved through Calhoun and Webster Counties.

A semi-stationary frontal boundary remained in place across southern Iowa while a secondary boundary was stretched from eastern South Dakota into northeast Iowa.  The airmass became very unstable with lifted indices falling to -10 to -15 C and most unstable CAPE rising to 5000 to 6000 J/kg.  Zero to 3 km CAPE rose to over 200 J/kg in northern Iowa by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose to the 80s to low 90s, as dew point values reached the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Wind fields were somewhat weak, however effective shear was in the 35 to 50 kt range by late afternoon.  The amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere rose to over 400 J/kg by late afternoon.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places initially.  The first was along the northern boundary.  However the severe storms with this round remained to the north of Iowa.  A second round formed north of the southern boundary along the edge of the mid level cap.  The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa.  The highest wind gust was 74 MPH, measured at an RWIS site near Ankeny in Polk County.  The wind damage was largely to trees and power lines, with spotty reports of small shed and minor building damage.  A semi-tractor trailer was blown off of Interstate 35 near Highway 210 in Story County.  The high winds ripped siding off of a house in Ankeny in Polk county, and windows were blown out of a house in Colfax in Jasper County.  Thunderstorms rekindled on the back side of the main thunderstorm area after sunrise.  A few storms became severe and produced 60 MPH winds and golf ball size hail as they moved from The Fort Dodge area into Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The power was out in Huxley. Pea size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass remained quite unstable across the state as a weak cold front approached from the northwest and the main short wave lifted northeast through the central U.S.  CAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg range with lifted indices between -8 and -9 C.  Thunderstorms formed over southern Iowa in the rich air with dew points in the mid 70s.  Downdraft CAPE was quite high at 1000 to 1200 J/kg.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was quite a bit less than the previous round, in the 400 to 800 J/kg range.  The column was significantly sheared with 45 to 65 kts of effective shear present.  Plenty of moisture was available with 2.2 inches of precipitable water noted just south of the thunderstorm inception area.  Thunderstorms fired by early afternoon over a good part of Iowa with the strongest in the south central and parts of central Iowa.  Initially, the storms produced sub-severe hail around nickle size.  As the afternoon progressed, the storms became stronger and resulted in high winds.  Winds of 68 MPH hit the ASOS at Ottumwa for example.  In addition, the storms produced heavy rainfall.  This produced flooded roads in some areas, especially in Warren and Madison Counties.

High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.

Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches. A semi-stationary frontal boundary remained in place across southern Iowa while a secondary boundary was stretched from eastern South Dakota into northeast Iowa.  The airmass became very unstable with lifted indices falling to -10 to -15 C and most unstable CAPE rising to 5000 to 6000 J/kg.  Zero to 3 km CAPE rose to over 200 J/kg in northern Iowa by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose to the 80s to low 90s, as dew point values reached the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Wind fields were somewhat weak, however effective shear was in the 35 to 50 kt range by late afternoon.  The amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere rose to over 400 J/kg by late afternoon.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places initially.  The first was along the northern boundary.  However the severe storms with this round remained to the north of Iowa.  A second round formed north of the southern boundary along the edge of the mid level cap.  The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa.  The highest wind gust was 74 MPH, measured at an RWIS site near Ankeny in Polk County.  The wind damage was largely to trees and power lines, with spotty reports of small shed and minor building damage.  A semi-tractor trailer was blown off of Interstate 35 near Highway 210 in Story County.  The high winds ripped siding off of a house in Ankeny in Polk county, and windows were blown out of a house in Colfax in Jasper County.  Thunderstorms rekindled on the back side of the main thunderstorm area after sunrise.  A few storms became severe and produced 60 MPH winds and golf ball size hail as they moved from The Fort Dodge area into Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two and one half foot diameter tree was downed.

Downdraft CAPE was between 1400 and 1700 J/kg and there was a significant amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere of 1000-1600 J/kg.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with 25 to 35 kts of effective shear.  Shear in the zero to 3 km layer was between 50 and 250 J/kg, resulting in considerable rotation of thunderstorm cells.  The freezing level was quite high, at 14,000 feet.  Considerable moisture was available through a deep layer of the atmosphere with precipitable water values of 1.75 to 2 inches.  Thunderstorms developed over the northwest part of Iowa by early afternoon.  The storms became severe and dropped southeast into the most unstable air to the east and south of the complex.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of high winds and hail.  Some of the hail reached golf ball in size, however with the warm atmospheric conditions much of the hail remained below severe levels.  High winds were reported all along the leading edge of the thunderstorm complex, with numerous reports in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  There were two areas where the most significant damage occurred.  The first was in the Waterloo, Cedar Falls, and Hudson areas of northeast Iowa.  A beta bow element pushed into the area resulting in more widespread wind damage.  Some of the damage was similar to what one might expect with a weak tornado.  The second area was over Dallas County.  High winds caused considerable damage from around Dallas Center, west to the county line.  An old airport field was hit by the winds with considerable damage to equipment and hangars.  Winds were estimated to be at least 80 MPH.  Later in the event, some of the storm cells collapsed with a local high wind event occurring in Hamilton County.  Two semi-tractor trailer trucks were blown over on Interstate 35 between mile post 162 and 165 in Franklin County.  There were numerous reports of wind damage around Franklin County.  Trees were blown down onto cars and houses.  Another semi-tractor trailer was blown off the highway just east of Perry on Highway 44 in Dallas County. Very heavy rain fell with the storms as well.  There were numerous reports of urban and small stream flooding.  Flash flooding took place in Dallas County, primarily in the Waukee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous branches broken off by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska during the night and early morning hours of the 24th.  The thunderstorms pushed northeast, then east into Iowa around sunrise.  The storms were elevated in nature by the time they reached Iowa.  One of the storms produced three quarter to one inch diameter hail as it moved through Calhoun and Webster Counties.

A semi-stationary frontal boundary remained in place across southern Iowa while a secondary boundary was stretched from eastern South Dakota into northeast Iowa.  The airmass became very unstable with lifted indices falling to -10 to -15 C and most unstable CAPE rising to 5000 to 6000 J/kg.  Zero to 3 km CAPE rose to over 200 J/kg in northern Iowa by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures rose to the 80s to low 90s, as dew point values reached the upper 60s to mid 70s.  Wind fields were somewhat weak, however effective shear was in the 35 to 50 kt range by late afternoon.  The amount of CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere rose to over 400 J/kg by late afternoon.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places initially.  The first was along the northern boundary.  However the severe storms with this round remained to the north of Iowa.  A second round formed north of the southern boundary along the edge of the mid level cap.  The storms became severe and produced high winds and hail.  Thunderstorms from farther west moved into Iowa later in the evening and became severe.  The storms produced a weak Derecho.  Widespread winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred with these storms, along with numerous reports of hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter.  The initial round of storms produced large hail and gusty winds from southwest into central Iowa.  The second round resulted in winds of 60 to 70 MPH and large hail from central into southeast Iowa.  The highest wind gust was 74 MPH, measured at an RWIS site near Ankeny in Polk County.  The wind damage was largely to trees and power lines, with spotty reports of small shed and minor building damage.  A semi-tractor trailer was blown off of Interstate 35 near Highway 210 in Story County.  The high winds ripped siding off of a house in Ankeny in Polk county, and windows were blown out of a house in Colfax in Jasper County.  Thunderstorms rekindled on the back side of the main thunderstorm area after sunrise.  A few storms became severe and produced 60 MPH winds and golf ball size hail as they moved from The Fort Dodge area into Story County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 to 6 inch tree branches were down and blown through the neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary extended from southwest into east central Iowa  The airmass was quite unstable with elevated CAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg.  The effective shear was 55-75 kts.  Meanwhile, a very strong low level jet was in place with flow across the frontal boundary of 50 to 75 kts.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 layer of the atmosphere was 600 to 700 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted across Iowa.  Many of them produced heavy rain and one half to three quarter inch diameter hail.  One storm over Cass County produced 2 1/2 inch diameter hail.  The hail smashed vehicle windshields west of Cumberland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front lifted northeast into Iowa during the night of the 6th into the daytime hours of the 7th.  The atmosphere was weakly sheared with only 20 kts of shear available.  By mid to late afternoon, it had become quite unstable with lifted indices in the -4 to -7 C range and CAPE in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range.  Significant CAPE was available in the low levels of the atmosphere with 150 to 250 J/kg in the lower 3 km.  A funnel cloud was produced by one of the cells in Story County south of Nevada.  The cell had 3 funnels reaching about 1/3 of the way to the ground and rotating around the cell.  These storms formed along the warm frontal boundary.  Some dime to nickel size hail was produced with these storms, along with heavy rainfall.  A total of 2.9 inches fell in 1 1/2 hours in Tama County with one of the cells, and 1.6 inches in about 45 minutes in Wapello County.  A weak cold front dropped into northern Iowa during the evening.  A complex of thunderstorms formed a weak bow echo as it moved into the state.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 200 to 500 J/kg range and downdraft CAPE was in the 700 to 1000 J/kg range.  The storms produced gusty winds and some hail, both below severe levels.  The winds were in the 35 to 50 MPH range with the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amount of 2.9 inches in 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the middle of the month resulted in the Iowa River at Marengo going above the moderate flood stage level of 15.5 feet on June 19th around 8 pm. It crested around 16.1 feet around 4 am June 20 and again around 16.2 feet around 730 am June 24. It finally fell below the moderate flood stage level around 230 am June 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains during the middle of the month resulted in the Iowa River at Marengo going above the moderate flood stage level of 15.5 feet on June 19th around 8 pm. It crested around 16.1 feet around 4 am June 20 and again around 16.2 feet around 730 am June 24. It finally fell below the moderate flood stage level around 230 am June 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were triggered along a warm front during the evening of June 17. Storm spotters observed funnel clouds and also reported hail up to the size of baseballs, which caused considerable damage to crops in some locations. Thunderstorm winds also caused damage to windows, siding and roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail a bit larger than golf ball size, with a single line of spikes, broke windows and damaged siding on a house. Also, a total of 4.8 inches of rain was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the central U.S. during the late afternoon of the 3rd through the early morning of the 4th.  The system resembled a winter type system as strong low pressure at the surface developed over Kansas and moved northeast into Missouri.  A large area of thunderstorms developed in the deformation zone north of the low.  A large part of Iowa was affected by the rain with a narrow band of 4 to 7 inch rainfall extending from near the Atlantic area to north of Lamoni. The heaviest report received was nearly 8 inches in Madison County at Orient.  Five to 6 inches of rain fell in parts of Wayne County.  The rain caused flooding of several areas with Clarke, Decatur, Madison, and Wayne Counties affected the most.  Numerous roads were closed by the water with flash flooding reported in Decatur County where 5 to 6 feet of water covered the road.  The flash flooding extended north into Clarke County.  Flash flooding also occurred in Appanoose County, where Highway 2 was flooded west of Centerville.  Flash flooding washed out gravel roads in Wayne and Adair Counties as well with debris thrown onto the roads.  Lightning struck a tree near a house in Creston.  The lightning blasted a hole in the ground at the base of the tree.  Branches and pieces were thrown into a nearby home.  Three screen doors were broken along with several windows in the house.  A nearby garage was also damaged.  Pieces of the tree were blown out about 70 feet from the initial strike.

A complicated weather situation unfolded during the day on the 14th into the evening.  A mesoscale convective system moved through the state during the previous night and left a considerable amount of debris cloudiness during the afternoon of the 14th.  In addition, a cap developed over the state during the afternoon.  A cold front approached Iowa from the northwest and had entered the northwest part of the state by evening.  The atmosphere became unstable during the late afternoon hours with most unstable CAPE near 4000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -9 to -12 C range.  There was considerable downdraft CAPE available, in excess of 1000 J/kg.  The CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 1000 to 1400 J/kg range by evening.  Shear values were in the 30 kt range initially.  Thunderstorms were slow to develop, but did eventually develop just ahead of the cold front in northwest Iowa.  A broken squall line developed by the early evening hours, and translated southeast into the state.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of hail in spite of the freezing level approaching 15,000 feet.  Spotty wind reports of around 60 MPH wind were received in north central and parts of central Iowa.  The storms produced hail in many locations.  For the most part, the hail was on the order of an inch or so in diameter.  The storms moved through Polk County shortly before midnight and produced very large hail.  Hail up to 3 inches in diameter was reported to the north and east of Johnston.  Considerable damage was done to cars with damaged wind shields and denting.  Damage also occurred to roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were broken out due to large hail at an apartment complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The instability and overall weather situation remained similar to what it was the previous evening.  Lifted indices were around -7 C, with downdraft CAPE values around 1000 J/kg.  Overall CAPE was in the 2000 to 4000 J/kg range.  Precipitable water values were around 1.75 inches with a deep warm cloud depth.  Freezing levels were in the 14,500 to 15,000 foot range.  A new round of thunderstorms moved into northwest Iowa on the heels of the overnight convection.  These storms were the last in a series of waves of mesoscale convective complexes that rolled across Iowa, and was 4th in the series.  As the storms turned southeast they produced high winds through portions of central Iowa.  Several reports of winds in the 60 to 65 MPH range were received in Story and Polk Counties.  Numerous trees were downed by the winds.  Three modular homes and 3 mobile homes were destroyed on a sale lot in Story County at Nevada.  Thunderstorms also dropped southeast over the northeast part of Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inch diameter tree branches were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure was located over Iowa during the afternoon of the 22nd.  Moisture was drawn into Iowa on the back side of the high and the atmosphere became unstable by mid to late afternoon.  Lifted indices fell to -1 to -3 C and CAPE increased to 1000 to 2000 J/kg.  The freezing level was relatively low at 12,000 feet and this set the stage for steep low level lapse rates above 8 C in the lowest 3 km.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere increased to 100 to 300 J/kg, while the downdraft CAPE increased to 700 to 900 J/kg. There was a fair amount of shear available with 30 to 45 kts present.  A scattered line of thunderstorms developed from north central into west central Iowa.  They became severe with hail the primary mode of severe weather, along with spotty high wind reports.  Hail was around an inch with the stronger storms, along with winds in excess of 60 MPH causing tree damage.  Hail covered the ground to a depth of several inches in Pocahontas County, causing considerable damage to trees and crops in the area.  The damage was from one cell as it moved south-southeast through Pocahontas, Humboldt, and Webster Counties.  The largest hail was about one and one quarter of an inch in diameter that occurred in Humboldt and Webster Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported by public three quarter to one inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorms that formed in the instability during the previous evening continued through the night as an elevated pulse type situation.  Weak low pressure developed over southeast Iowa, giving a slight enhancement in the low level convergence during the predawn hours.  One thunderstorm became severe as it passed southeast near Chariton in Lucas County.  The cell dropped three quarter inch diameter hail as it moved through Charition.  About 2 inches of rain fell in under one half hour as well, causing creeks to reach bankfull stage.  The storm became severe in Appanoose County and dropped one inch diameter hail north of Plano.  Hail fell west of Rathbun Lake and cut a three quarter mile wide swath.  Damage was done to a pickup truck and crops were destroyed along the path.  Hail accumulated to the point that hail was still on the ground 8 hours after the event.

Hail up to 3 inches in diameter was reported to the north and east of Johnston.  Considerable damage was done to cars with damaged wind shields and denting.  Damage also occurred to roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported at the intersection of NW Oaktree Drive and Timberbrooke lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the central U.S. during the late afternoon of the 3rd through the early morning of the 4th.  The system resembled a winter type system as strong low pressure at the surface developed over Kansas and moved northeast into Missouri.  A large area of thunderstorms developed in the deformation zone north of the low.  A large part of Iowa was affected by the rain with a narrow band of 4 to 7 inch rainfall extending from near the Atlantic area to north of Lamoni. The heaviest report received was nearly 8 inches in Madison County at Orient.  Five to 6 inches of rain fell in parts of Wayne County.  The rain caused flooding of several areas with Clarke, Decatur, Madison, and Wayne Counties affected the most.  Numerous roads were closed by the water with flash flooding reported in Decatur County where 5 to 6 feet of water covered the road.  The flash flooding extended north into Clarke County.  Flash flooding also occurred in Appanoose County, where Highway 2 was flooded west of Centerville.  Flash flooding washed out gravel roads in Wayne and Adair Counties as well with debris thrown onto the roads.  Lightning struck a tree near a house in Creston.  The lightning blasted a hole in the ground at the base of the tree.  Branches and pieces were thrown into a nearby home.  Three screen doors were broken along with several windows in the house.  A nearby garage was also damaged.  Pieces of the tree were blown out about 70 feet from the initial strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 2 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure was located over Iowa during the afternoon of the 22nd.  Moisture was drawn into Iowa on the back side of the high and the atmosphere became unstable by mid to late afternoon.  Lifted indices fell to -1 to -3 C and CAPE increased to 1000 to 2000 J/kg.  The freezing level was relatively low at 12,000 feet and this set the stage for steep low level lapse rates above 8 C in the lowest 3 km.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere increased to 100 to 300 J/kg, while the downdraft CAPE increased to 700 to 900 J/kg. There was a fair amount of shear available with 30 to 45 kts present.  A scattered line of thunderstorms developed from north central into west central Iowa.  They became severe with hail the primary mode of severe weather, along with spotty high wind reports.  Hail was around an inch with the stronger storms, along with winds in excess of 60 MPH causing tree damage.  Hail covered the ground to a depth of several inches in Pocahontas County, causing considerable damage to trees and crops in the area.  The damage was from one cell as it moved south-southeast through Pocahontas, Humboldt, and Webster Counties.  The largest hail was about one and one quarter of an inch in diameter that occurred in Humboldt and Webster Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree branches blown down and blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The instability and overall weather situation remained similar to what it was the previous evening.  Lifted indices were around -7 C, with downdraft CAPE values around 1000 J/kg.  Overall CAPE was in the 2000 to 4000 J/kg range.  Precipitable water values were around 1.75 inches with a deep warm cloud depth.  Freezing levels were in the 14,500 to 15,000 foot range.  A new round of thunderstorms moved into northwest Iowa on the heels of the overnight convection.  These storms were the last in a series of waves of mesoscale convective complexes that rolled across Iowa, and was 4th in the series.  As the storms turned southeast they produced high winds through portions of central Iowa.  Several reports of winds in the 60 to 65 MPH range were received in Story and Polk Counties.  Numerous trees were downed by the winds.  Three modular homes and 3 mobile homes were destroyed on a sale lot in Story County at Nevada.  Thunderstorms also dropped southeast over the northeast part of Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inch diameter tree branch fell down on a house causing roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complicated weather situation unfolded during the day on the 14th into the evening.  A mesoscale convective system moved through the state during the previous night and left a considerable amount of debris cloudiness during the afternoon of the 14th.  In addition, a cap developed over the state during the afternoon.  A cold front approached Iowa from the northwest and had entered the northwest part of the state by evening.  The atmosphere became unstable during the late afternoon hours with most unstable CAPE near 4000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -9 to -12 C range.  There was considerable downdraft CAPE available, in excess of 1000 J/kg.  The CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 1000 to 1400 J/kg range by evening.  Shear values were in the 30 kt range initially.  Thunderstorms were slow to develop, but did eventually develop just ahead of the cold front in northwest Iowa.  A broken squall line developed by the early evening hours, and translated southeast into the state.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of hail in spite of the freezing level approaching 15,000 feet.  Spotty wind reports of around 60 MPH wind were received in north central and parts of central Iowa.  The storms produced hail in many locations.  For the most part, the hail was on the order of an inch or so in diameter.  The storms moved through Polk County shortly before midnight and produced very large hail.  Hail up to 3 inches in diameter was reported to the north and east of Johnston.  Considerable damage was done to cars with damaged wind shields and denting.  Damage also occurred to roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail measured 2.25 inches by 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front traveled southeast across the state during the afternoon and evening hours of the 24th.  The airmass was quite capped with 700 mb temperatures in the 10 to 13 C range.  Though afternoon highs reached the mid 80s to low 90s, with dew point readings in the upper 60s to low 70s, this was not sufficient to break the cap in most cases.  The atmosphere was moderately sheared with 40 to 50 kts of shear.  Most unstable CAPE values were around 4000 J/kg with a lifted index around -8 C.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was between 500 and 800 J/kg, with 800 to 1100 J/kg of downdraft CAPE available.  The freezing level was quite high in the 13,000 to 14,000 foot range.  Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon over northeast Iowa.  By the late afternoon hours, the storms began to develop southwestward along the front, but were not able to extend very far into the cap.  Many of the storms were able to produce sub-severe hail, but only one produced one inch diameter hail as it moved through the Gilman area of Marshall County.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the evening of June 17, producing 3 to 5 inches of rain within a couple of hours. Localized flash flooding affected areas near the town of Lawler, where water covered several roads and also washed out some farmer's dikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered roads in several locations, including County Road V56 and Vanderbilt Avenue, as well as along 190th Street. In addition, some farmer's dikes were reported washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of June 23, a cluster of thunderstorms producing wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph affected locations from Charles City (Floyd County) to Oelwein (Fayette County).

Relatively wet weather prevailed over much of Iowa for the first one-half of July but the second half of the month was much drier in most areas. Monthly rain totals varied from 2.03 inches at Newton to 8.95 inches at Anamosa.  Heavy rainfall occurred over central into south central Iowa during the day on the 3rd into the 4th.  This caused several rivers to rise above flood stage.  These included the Chariton, Thompson, and South Fork Chariton Rivers.  The flooding was relatively minor and affected agricultural lands for the most part.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorms that formed in the instability during the previous evening continued through the night as an elevated pulse type situation.  Weak low pressure developed over southeast Iowa, giving a slight enhancement in the low level convergence during the predawn hours.  One thunderstorm became severe as it passed southeast near Chariton in Lucas County.  The cell dropped three quarter inch diameter hail as it moved through Charition.  About 2 inches of rain fell in under one half hour as well, causing creeks to reach bankfull stage.  The storm became severe in Appanoose County and dropped one inch diameter hail north of Plano.  Hail fell west of Rathbun Lake and cut a three quarter mile wide swath.  Damage was done to a pickup truck and crops were destroyed along the path.  Hail accumulated to the point that hail was still on the ground 8 hours after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were triggered along a warm front during the evening of June 17. Storm spotters observed funnel clouds and also reported hail up to the size of baseballs, which caused considerable damage to crops in some locations. Thunderstorm winds also caused damage to windows, siding and roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported at 190th Street and Glass Avenue by several groups, including spotters and the broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed in western Nebraska during the late evening of June 22nd moved into western Iowa around noon on the 23rd.  The storms created an outflow boundary which collided with very unstable conditions, temperatures of 90 to 95 degrees and dew point temperatures in the upper 70s.  This caused new thunderstorm development along and east of the Missouri River north of Omaha by mid afternoon.  The storms were mainly wet micro-burst producers although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 mph knocked down numerous 3 to 4 inch limbs and split at least several other larger trees.  One large branch was toppled onto a moving pickup severely damaging its front end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated area of thunderstorms developed just south of Waterloo, IA during the late evening of May 31.  One thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Mount Auburn, IA around 10:53 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail fell in Mount Auburn, IA around 1053 pm on May 31.  This report was by a weather spotter for KCRG-TV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the central U.S. during the late afternoon of the 3rd through the early morning of the 4th.  The system resembled a winter type system as strong low pressure at the surface developed over Kansas and moved northeast into Missouri.  A large area of thunderstorms developed in the deformation zone north of the low.  A large part of Iowa was affected by the rain with a narrow band of 4 to 7 inch rainfall extending from near the Atlantic area to north of Lamoni. The heaviest report received was nearly 8 inches in Madison County at Orient.  Five to 6 inches of rain fell in parts of Wayne County.  The rain caused flooding of several areas with Clarke, Decatur, Madison, and Wayne Counties affected the most.  Numerous roads were closed by the water with flash flooding reported in Decatur County where 5 to 6 feet of water covered the road.  The flash flooding extended north into Clarke County.  Flash flooding also occurred in Appanoose County, where Highway 2 was flooded west of Centerville.  Flash flooding washed out gravel roads in Wayne and Adair Counties as well with debris thrown onto the roads.  Lightning struck a tree near a house in Creston.  The lightning blasted a hole in the ground at the base of the tree.  Branches and pieces were thrown into a nearby home.  Three screen doors were broken along with several windows in the house.  A nearby garage was also damaged.  Pieces of the tree were blown out about 70 feet from the initial strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in the 24 hour period was 4.74 inches, with an additional .25 inches since observation.  Most rain has been since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The instability and overall weather situation remained similar to what it was the previous evening.  Lifted indices were around -7 C, with downdraft CAPE values around 1000 J/kg. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure was located over Iowa during the afternoon of the 22nd.  Moisture was drawn into Iowa on the back side of the high and the atmosphere became unstable by mid to late afternoon.  Lifted indices fell to -1 to -3 C and CAPE increased to 1000 to 2000 J/kg.  The freezing level was relatively low at 12,000 feet and this set the stage for steep low level lapse rates above 8 C in the lowest 3 km.  CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere increased to 100 to 300 J/kg, while the downdraft CAPE increased to 700 to 900 J/kg. There was a fair amount of shear available with 30 to 45 kts present.  A scattered line of thunderstorms developed from north central into west central Iowa.  They became severe with hail the primary mode of severe weather, along with spotty high wind reports.  Hail was around an inch with the stronger storms, along with winds in excess of 60 MPH causing tree damage.  Hail covered the ground to a depth of several inches in Pocahontas County, causing considerable damage to trees and crops in the area.  The damage was from one cell as it moved south-southeast through Pocahontas, Humboldt, and Webster Counties.  The largest hail was about one and one quarter of an inch in diameter that occurred in Humboldt and Webster Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak convergence zone dropped southeast into Iowa during the afternoon.  Residual moisture was enough for thunderstorms to develop.  Lifted indices fell to -6 C, with CAPE around 2000 J/kg.  The freezing level was around 13,000 feet.  Several of the storms produced small hail and very heavy rainfall.  One of them dropped quarter size hail in the Dike area.  A few cold air funnel clouds were reported over eastern and southern Butler County as the storms receded.

A complicated weather situation unfolded during the day on the 14th into the evening.  A mesoscale convective system moved through the state during the previous night and left a considerable amount of debris cloudiness during the afternoon of the 14th.  In addition, a cap developed over the state during the afternoon.  A cold front approached Iowa from the northwest and had entered the northwest part of the state by evening.  The atmosphere became unstable during the late afternoon hours with most unstable CAPE near 4000 J/kg and lifted indices in the -9 to -12 C range.  There was considerable downdraft CAPE available, in excess of 1000 J/kg.  The CAPE in the -10 to -30 C layer of the atmosphere was in the 1000 to 1400 J/kg range by evening.  Shear values were in the 30 kt range initially.  Thunderstorms were slow to develop, but did eventually develop just ahead of the cold front in northwest Iowa.  A broken squall line developed by the early evening hours, and translated southeast into the state.  The primary mode of severe weather with this event was in the form of hail in spite of the freezing level approaching 15,000 feet.  Spotty wind reports of around 60 MPH wind were received in north central and parts of central Iowa.  The storms produced hail in many locations.  For the most part, the hail was on the order of an inch or so in diameter.  The storms moved through Polk County shortly before midnight and produced very large hail.  Hail up to 3 inches in diameter was reported to the north and east of Johnston.  Considerable damage was done to cars with damaged wind shields and denting.  Damage also occurred to roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind blew over an 8 X 8 unanchored metal shed.  Estimated 12 inch diameter tree branches were down and power poles were bent over.
